factual-accuracy.requirement: States that in a 2019 cross-sectional survey of Rohingya refugee women in Camp-4, Cox’s Bazar, approximately 71.6% of respondents reported attending at least four antenatal care visits (ANC4+)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that a single-camp cross-sectional study in Camp-4 reported an ANC 4+ completion rate of 71.6% (shown in the ANC 4+ table for Cox’s Bazar and reiterated in the data matrix).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States that in a retrospective analysis of Thailand–Myanmar border camp maternal health data through 2018 (including Mae La), more than 90% of pregnant women attended ≥4 antenatal care visits (ANC4+)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not state that a retrospective analysis of Thailand–Myanmar border camp maternal health data through 2018 (including Mae La) found >90% ANC4+ attendance. It only mentions a proxy finding that >90% ANC4+ occurred in Mae Ra Ma Luang and Mae La Oon (2000–2018) and repeatedly notes no Mae La-specific ANC4+ data for 2017–2023.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Rohingya facility births/SBA started at 12-22% in 2018 (or as low as 4% in late 2017 emergency period)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports facility-based delivery at 3.9% in Nov–Dec 2017 and 22% in Feb 2018, but it does not state that facility births/SBA started at 12–22% in 2018 nor explicitly note a “as low as 4% in late 2017 emergency period” framing. The specific 12–22% 2018 starting range is not provided as such.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States at least three distinct year–percentage pairs documenting facility-based delivery among Rohingya refugees in Cox’s Bazar, including one estimate ≤15% in 2018, one intermediate estimate between 40–60% from 2019–2020, and one estimate ≥75% from 2022–2023
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides multiple year–percentage pairs for facility-based delivery in Cox’s Bazar: 22% (Feb 2018, not ≤15%), 35% (2019), 53.2% (2020), and 82% (Q3 2023)/85% (early 2024). However, it does not include any 2018 estimate at or below 15%, so the required low 2018 pair is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mae La facility births/SBA at 75-80% with stable mature service delivery
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not state that Mae La facility births or skilled birth attendance are at 75–80%, nor does it describe this as stable mature service delivery. Instead, it repeatedly notes that no Mae La-specific SBA data for 2017–2023 is available and provides only proxy/historical information.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Rohingya NMR as 27 per 1,000 live births for September 2017-December 2018 period with most deaths occurring at home
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Rohingya neonatal mortality for Sept 2017–Dec 2018 was 27.0 per 1,000 live births and adds that 76.3% of neonatal deaths occurred at home or in the community, satisfying both the rate and location-of-death elements.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mae La NMR declined to approximately 10-11 per 1,000 live births by 2011 and remained stable
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says Mae La neonatal mortality declined from 21.8 to 10.7 per 1,000 live births (2008–2011), but it does not state it declined to approximately 10–11 per 1,000 by 2011 and remained stable thereafter. It provides no claim of post-2011 stability for Mae La NMR.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Rohingya MMR approximately 200-230 per 100,000 live births in 2022-2023 period (accounting for methodology variation between 200 and 295)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives Rohingya MMR figures of 229.17 (2022) and 179–295 (2023), plus other estimates up to 446–447, but it does not state an approximate 200–230 range for the 2022–2023 period. It also does not frame the methodology variation as “between 200 and 295” while centering ~200–230.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States that the maternal mortality ratio among Rohingya women in Cox’s Bazar refugee camps was reported at 295 per 100,000 live births in 2023 according to health sector reporting
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions an MMR estimate of 295 per 100,000 live births for 2023, but attributes it to “UNICEF data” and does not state it was “among Rohingya women in Cox’s Bazar refugee camps” nor that it was “according to health sector reporting.” The required phrasing and source attribution to health sector reporting are missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mae La or SMRU Thai-resident refugee cohorts had low MMR (~79-126 per 100,000) with zero malaria-related maternal deaths since 2005
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes proxy data for Thai-Myanmar border refugee camps, stating malaria-related maternal mortality fell to zero by 2005 and citing non–Mae La MMR values (e.g., 126.5/100,000). However, it does not state that Mae La or SMRU Thai-resident refugee cohorts had low MMR around 79–126 per 100,000, nor does it explicitly say zero malaria-related maternal deaths since 2005 for Mae La/SMRU cohorts.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States that obstetric hemorrhage (including postpartum hemorrhage) is reported as a leading direct cause category of maternal deaths in the Rohingya Cox’s Bazar MPMSR surveillance reports, and states that postpartum hemorrhage contributes to maternal deaths in Thailand–Myanmar border refugee/migrant settings
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that MPMSR surveillance reports for Cox’s Bazar list obstetric hemorrhage as the leading cause category of maternal deaths (with yearly percentages). However, it does not state that postpartum hemorrhage contributes to maternal deaths in Thailand–Myanmar border refugee/migrant settings, instead repeatedly noting no Mae La cause-of-death data.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States routine PPH incidence per 1,000 births is not publicly reported or available for either setting
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that no population-based PPH incidence rate (e.g., per 1,000 deliveries) was found for either setting in 2017–2023 and reiterates that neither setting reports population-based PPH incidence rates. This directly conveys that routine PPH incidence per 1,000 births is not publicly available for both Cox’s Bazar and Mae La.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States that health care in the Cox’s Bazar Rohingya camps is at least 2 of the following: delivered through a coordinated humanitarian partner system (WHO-led Health Sector with NGO/UN partners), includes primary health care centers, includes field hospitals, or explicitly notes the presence of 24/7 PHCC services.
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states Cox’s Bazar healthcare is coordinated through a WHO-led Health Sector with multiple NGO/UN partners and describes a network of health posts and primary health centres (PHC/PHCC-level services). These two elements satisfy the requirement of at least two listed features.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes Mae La as NGO primary care (SMRU, PU-AMI, or Malteser) integrated with Thai Ministry of Public Health for referrals
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Mae La’s model as SMRU providing maternal-child primary care in-camp with referrals to Thai provincial hospitals (e.g., Tha Song Yang, Mae Ramat) under Thai Ministry of Public Health collaboration, contrasting it with NGO field-hospital delivery in Cox’s Bazar.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Cox's Bazar has >400 midwives active in response or approximately 213 midwives at UNFPA facilities during 2021-2022
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions UNFPA midwives as “approximately 283 midwives supported by UNFPA in 2023” and earlier “~35 midwives deployed,” but it does not state that Cox’s Bazar has >400 midwives active in the response or that there were approximately 213 midwives at UNFPA facilities during 2021–2022. Those specific threshold figures are absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States at least one of the following: that no published peer-reviewed/authoritative reports provide a midwife-to-population ratio for Cox’s Bazar refugee camps or host communities, OR explicitly avoids reporting a numeric ratio for midwives in this setting
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states an “Explicit data gap” that no specific midwife-to-population ratio is published for the Rohingya camps and also for Mae La camp. Although it provides a rough illustrative calculation for Cox’s Bazar, it clearly notes this is not a published ratio and is incomplete.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mae La has approximately 20 camp-resident SBAs or describes small stable established SBA workforce mentored by SMRU
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Mae La’s maternal health services via SMRU and mentions SMRU birth attendant training, but it does not state that Mae La has approximately 20 camp-resident SBAs nor explicitly describe a small stable established SBA workforce mentored by SMRU.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes Cox's Bazar EmONC access including on-camp Friendship CEmONC hospital and UNFPA referral hubs launched 2019-2020 providing 24/7 transport
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Cox’s Bazar referral transport (e.g., Matri Sheba, IOM ambulances, 24-hour hotline) but does not mention an on-camp Friendship CEmONC hospital or UNFPA referral hubs launched in 2019–2020 providing 24/7 transport. Those specific EmONC access elements are absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Mae La requires referral to Thai MoPH hospitals for C-sections at distance of 60-80 km or typically under 2 hours travel
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that Mae La lacks C-section capacity and refers emergency cases to Thai provincial hospitals, but it does not state that C-sections require referral specifically to Thai MoPH hospitals at a distance of 60–80 km or typically under 2 hours travel. No travel time or distance figures are provided for Mae La referrals.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States TBAs in both settings transitioned from delivery providers to mediators, companions, or community mobilizers rather than clinical attendants
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes TBAs in Cox’s Bazar being “trained to refer women… rather than conduct home deliveries” and “transformed into SRH Referral Assistants,” and in Mae La being used in “Mother Support Groups” and training roles, indicating a shift from clinical attendants to mediators/companions/community mobilizers in both settings.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes 2021 UNFPA funding impact was mixed or limited evidence with services continuing to improve, and more acute disruptions documented in 2023-2025 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly notes that evidence for a clear causal impact from the 2021 UNFPA funding reduction is mixed/limited, citing continued improvement in service utilization despite constraints. It also documents more acute, well-described disruptions occurring later (e.g., 2023 closures and especially 2025 large-scale facility shutdowns), aligning with the criterion’s 2023–2025 emphasis.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Shows at least 3 intermediate time points for key indicator progression (e.g., facility births 32%→52.7%→60%→82%) not just baseline and endpoint
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides multiple intermediate time points for indicator progression, especially for Cox’s Bazar skilled birth attendance/facility delivery (e.g., 3.9% in 2017 → 22% in 2018 → 32% in late 2018 → 35% in 2019 → 53.2% in 2020 → 67% in 2021 → 82% in 2023 → 85% in 2024). This exceeds the baseline-and-endpoint-only requirement.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly explains how specific inputs (midwife scale-up, referral hubs) drove outcome changes rather than merely correlating them
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response goes beyond correlation by describing mechanisms linking inputs to outcomes, e.g., TBA training/“referral assistants” and midwife mentorship as drivers of increased facility births, and transport/referral delays and lack of blood transfusion capacity as pathways to persistent hemorrhage-related mortality. It also explicitly qualifies causality around the 2021 funding reduction rather than implying simple association.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Addresses apparent contradictions in source data and explains why different figures appear (methodology, scope, or temporal differences)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly flags contradictory MMR figures for Cox’s Bazar (e.g., 179 vs 295 vs 446–447) and explains they differ due to estimation methods, surveillance completeness, and differing time periods. It also distinguishes proxy vs camp-specific data (e.g., Mae La vs Mae La Oon/Mae Ra Ma Luang) and notes indicator definition/scope issues (facility delivery as SBA proxy; PPH as cause-of-death share vs incidence).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Clearly separates 2017-2023 data from post-period updates and flags when cited figures fall outside requested timeframe
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly distinguishes the 2017–2023 analysis window from later information by explicitly labeling items like “early 2024,” “May 2025,” and “post-February 2025” as outside the timeframe. It also flags proxy/historical figures (e.g., 1986–2024, 2000–2018, pre-2017) as not Mae La-specific or out-of-period.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains how specific delivery model features (parallel vs integrated, CEmONC location, referral design) produce different outcome patterns
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response compares Cox’s Bazar’s NGO-field-hospital model versus Mae La’s Thai MOPH-integrated referral model, detailing referral transport constraints, in-camp vs referred C-section capacity, and TBA cultural mediation approaches. It also links these delivery-model features to observed patterns like rising facility deliveries but persistent mortality in Cox’s Bazar, attributing this to referral delays and quality-of-care gaps.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies EmONC signal functions available, facility counts/types, or referral pathway details beyond general model description
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides referral pathway details beyond a general model description, including specific ambulance numbers (e.g., IOM’s 10 ambulances), named referral services (Matri Sheba), referral volumes (10,492 EmOC referrals in 2023), and named Thai referral hospitals (Tha Song Yang, Mae Ramat). It also specifies facility/actor counts (49 partners, 458 facilities).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Questions or qualifies the 2021 funding reduction claim with evidence rather than accepting premise, noting what data support or contradict it
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly qualifies the 2021 UNFPA funding reduction claim, citing mixed/contradictory trends (improving facility deliveries vs persistently high mortality) and stating the evidence does not support a clear linear causal relationship. It also notes missing Mae La-specific 2021 UNFPA reduction data and frames Mae La funding pressures as diffuse rather than a 2021 shock.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Treats Cox's Bazar parallel humanitarian system and Mae La integrated Thai MoPH model as functionally equivalent without distinguishing their structural differences
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts Cox’s Bazar as a multi-actor NGO/UN-coordinated field-hospital system with Mae La as a smaller, protracted model involving SMRU/IRC and referral into Thai MoPH/provincial hospitals. It includes a comparison table and narrative emphasizing differences in coordination, referral pathways, C-section capacity, and system maturity rather than treating them as equivalent.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents health metrics with denominators, time periods, and appropriate qualifiers (e.g., "per 1,000 live births," "2017-2023")
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly reports metrics with explicit denominators and time periods, such as MMR “per 100,000 live births,” neonatal mortality “per 1,000 live births,” and multiple period qualifiers like “2017–2023,” “Sept 2017–Dec 2018,” and “Q1 2023.”

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses systematic side-by-side comparison format for settings/indicators with clear headings or tables
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly uses side-by-side comparison tables with clear headings (e.g., separate tables for ANC 4+, SBA, PPH, NMR, MMR, plus a delivery-model comparison table and a data availability matrix). These tables systematically contrast Cox’s Bazar versus Mae La across years and indicators.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains scholarly analytical tone and explicitly flags data gaps, methodological variations, or uncertainty in evidence
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, scholarly analytical tone throughout and repeatedly and explicitly flags uncertainty and methodological limitations (e.g., “critical data gap,” “methodological notes,” “proxy data,” “not calculable,” and “causal qualification” regarding funding impacts). It also highlights indicator definition issues (PPH incidence vs share of maternal deaths) and source-to-source variation in MMR estimates.

presentation-quality.requirement: Repeats the same health indicator, statistic, or key finding in more than two separate sections without adding new context
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeats the same key findings (e.g., “Mae La has no published 2017–2023 camp-specific data” and “PPH incidence data not available; only % of maternal deaths”) across multiple sections such as the Executive Summary, indicator subsections, data matrix, limitations, and conclusions without adding materially new context each time.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents Cox's Bazar vs Mae La indicator comparisons in narrative paragraphs only, with no tables, bullet lists, or structured side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple structured comparison tables (e.g., year-by-year indicator tables and side-by-side dimension tables) and also includes bullet lists in the executive summary. Therefore, it is not presented in narrative paragraphs only.

citation-quality.requirement: References UNHCR iRHIS (integrated Refugee Health Information System) dashboards, camp-level health data, or UNHCR health surveillance systems
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references UNHCR health surveillance systems by citing a “UNHCR HIS Site Report 2018 - Mae La Oon, Thailand” with a his.unhcr.org link, which is part of UNHCR’s health information system dashboards/site reports.

citation-quality.requirement: References MSF (Médecins Sans Frontières) operational reports, field observations, or humanitarian program documentation
citation-quality.explanation: The response references MSF documentation by citing an MSF Science Portal item (“Why mothers give birth at home…”) and also lists MSF among Health Sector partners, indicating use of MSF operational/field reporting sources.

citation-quality.requirement: References MPMSR (Maternal and Perinatal Death Surveillance and Response) audits, WHO/MPMSR mortality reviews, or maternal death surveillance data
citation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly references MPMSR audits and annual reports (2020–2023) and uses MPMSR maternal death surveillance data (e.g., causes of death and maternal deaths by year) for Cox’s Bazar.

citation-quality.requirement: References UNFPA operational reports, facility-level data, maternal health program documentation, or reproductive health service statistics
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple UNFPA documents (e.g., MPMSR Annual Reports 2020–2023 and SRH Working Group bulletins) and includes UNHCR HIS material (e.g., a 2018 HIS site report), which are operational/programmatic sources and service statistics.

citation-quality.requirement: References SMRU (Shoklo Malaria Research Unit) research publications, Thai border camp health program data, or Mae La clinical cohort studies
citation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly references SMRU (Shoklo Malaria Research Unit) and cites SMRU-related cohort work and Mae La intrapartum care studies, including an SMRU refugee/migrant pregnancy cohort profile and Mae La clinic research.

citation-quality.requirement: References household surveys, camp-specific assessments, or population-based data collection (e.g., KAP surveys, coverage surveys)
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple population-based and household survey-type data sources, including a prospective surveillance study across 29 camps for neonatal mortality and cross-sectional camp studies for ANC/SBA, as well as routine HIS-style surveillance (MPMSR) and sector coverage bulletins.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites only news articles, blog posts, or promotional materials as primary evidence for epidemiological indicators without surveillance data or operational reports
citation-quality.explanation: The response relies heavily on surveillance and operational sources such as UNHCR HIS site reports, UNFPA MPMSR annual reports, WHO Health Sector bulletins, and SRH Working Group bulletins, alongside peer-reviewed studies. While it includes some news/blog-like items (e.g., CNN, Wikipedia, Facebook), these are not the only primary evidence and are not used in place of surveillance/operational data.
